one_line_takeaway: Refusal in 13 open-source chat models collapses onto a single residual-stream direction that a gradient-free, harmful-data-free rank-one weight edit can strip out for under $5, matching prompt-specific jailbreaks while mostly preserving capabilities, underscoring the brittleness of current safety fine-tuning.
key_finding: A single direction controls refusal across all 13 models: ablating it drops refusal and safety scores sharply (Figure 1) and adding it makes models refuse harmless requests like a yoga-benefits question (Figures 3-4). The rank-one weight-orthogonalization jailbreak (ORTHO) is competitive with much more expensive methods. On HarmBench (default system prompt), ORTHO reaches 79.2% ASR on Qwen 7B and 84.3% on Qwen 14B, on par with prompt-specific GCG (79.5% and 83.5%); on Llama-2 7B it is 22.6% with the system prompt but 79.9% without it, exposing strong system-prompt sensitivity for Llama-2 that Qwen lacks. Capabilities are largely preserved: for the largest model per family, MMLU/ARC/GSM8K stay within about 1.5 points of baseline, but TruthfulQA consistently drops (e.g. Gemma 7B -2.4, Yi 34B -3.5, Llama-3 70B -2.3). Mechanistically, an adversarial suffix suppresses the refusal direction's expression down to harmless-instruction levels by shifting the top heads' attention from the instruction region to the suffix region.
core_contribution: The paper shows empirically that refusal behavior in safety fine-tuned chat LLMs is mediated by a single one-dimensional subspace in the residual stream, demonstrated across 13 open-source chat models (1.8B-72B). For each model the authors extract one 'refusal direction' via difference-in-means over contrastive harmful/harmless instruction activations, and show it is both necessary and sufficient for refusal: ablating the direction (directional ablation) removes the model's ability to refuse harmful prompts, while adding it induces refusal on harmless prompts. Leveraging this, they introduce a novel white-box jailbreak: a rank-one weight orthogonalization edit (Eq. 5) that permanently removes the model's ability to write the refusal direction, disabling refusal without gradient optimization or any harmful-completion examples, while preserving general capabilities. They also give a preliminary mechanistic account (on Qwen 1.8B) of how adversarial suffixes work: the suffix suppresses expression of the refusal direction by hijacking the attention of the top DFA-contributing heads away from the instruction region toward the suffix region.
threat_model: White-box adversary with full access to open-source model weights who wants to remove safety refusal (uncensor the model). The attacker computes the refusal direction from a small set of harmful/harmless instructions (no harmful completions needed) and either ablates it at inference time or bakes the removal directly into the weights via a rank-one orthogonalization. The paper frames this as demonstrating the brittleness of current open-source safety fine-tuning: the safety mechanism collapses to a single direction that costs <$5 of compute to strip from a 70B model. The suffix analysis additionally illuminates the mechanism behind existing black-box GCG-style adversarial-suffix attacks.
limitations: Authors state findings may not generalize to untested or larger/proprietary models; the direction-extraction method is heuristic and admittedly 'likely not optimal' (an existence proof, not an optimal-extraction study); the adversarial-suffix analysis is restricted to a single model (Qwen 1.8B) and a single suffix, so it is not a comprehensive mechanistic account; coherence is hard to measure and every metric used is considered flawed; the semantic meaning of the 'refusal direction' is unclear (could be 'harm'/'danger' rather than 'refusal'). The refusal score is naive substring matching (does not assess coherence or actual harmfulness, hence the complementary Llama Guard safety score). The attack is white-box only, requiring weight access. TruthfulQA degradation indicates the edit is not perfectly surgical.

datasets_used: AdvBench, MaliciousInstruct, TDC2023, HarmBench (harmful instruction training pool Dharmful; 128 train / 32 val)
Alpaca (harmless instruction pool Dharmless; 128 train / 32 val)
JailbreakBench (100 harmful instructions, used for the ablation refusal/safety evaluation in Fig 1)
Alpaca (100 harmless instructions for the activation-addition induced-refusal evaluation, Fig 3)
HarmBench test set (159 standard behaviors, ASR benchmark)
MMLU, ARC, GSM8K, TruthfulQA (capability evals; plus WinoGrande and TinyHellaSwag in appendix)
quantitative_results: HarmBench ASR (default system prompt) - ORTHO: Llama-2 7B 22.6% (79.9% no system prompt), Llama-2 13B 6.9% (61.0%), Llama-2 70B 4.4% (62.9%), Qwen 7B 79.2% (74.8%), Qwen 14B 84.3% (74.8%), Qwen 72B 78.0% (79.2%)
ORTHO vs prompt-specific GCG: Qwen 7B 79.2% vs 79.5%; Qwen 14B 84.3% vs 83.5% (on par)
Capability change (orthogonalized / baseline / delta) - Gemma 7B: MMLU 51.8/51.7 (+0.1), ARC 51.7/51.5 (+0.2), GSM8K 31.3/32.0 (-0.7), TruthfulQA 44.7/47.1 (-2.4)
TruthfulQA drops consistently for all orthogonalized models; MMLU/ARC/GSM8K within 99% CIs except Qwen 7B and Yi 34B
Jailbreaking a 70B model via this method costs less than $5 of compute
Suffix mechanistic analysis: top-8 attention heads by DFA on Qwen 1.8B; adversarial suffix suppresses refusal-direction expression to harmless-instruction levels
baselines_compared: Random suffix and no-suffix conditions (for the adversarial-suffix mechanistic analysis)
Baseline unmodified models (for capability retention comparison)
